perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall use commercially reasonable efforts to: (a) amend, supplement, or modify each Management-Ownership Agreement, as necessary or appropriate, to be consistent with, and provide for each transaction and other matter contemplated by, this Agreement; and (b) cooperate with each other Party, including, without limitation, as reasonably requested by any Party to jointly and mutually determine how best to structure and facilitate the Offer Matters and each agreement and transaction contemplated hereby and maximize value for all concerned Parties, taking into account speed, timing, deal certainty, administrative convenience, and applicable tax, legal, and business considerations and all other relevant issues.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall endeavor to: (a) consider amendments, supplements, or modifications to each Management-Ownership Agreement, as they individually deem suitable, to align with the general intent of this Agreement; and (b) occasionally cooperate with each other Party, as they find convenient, to structure and facilitate the Offer Matters, aiming to enhance value for themselves, considering only their individual business perspectives.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications replace 'commercially reasonable efforts' with weaker terms like 'endeavor,' 'consider,' 'deem suitable,' 'occasionally cooperate,' and 'find convenient.' The original text requires a standard of reasonable effort and joint determination, while the modified text makes the actions discretionary and self-serving. The change from 'maximize value for all concerned Parties' to 'enhance value for themselves, considering only their individual business perspectives' introduces a conflict of interest and undermines the collaborative intent. This introduces ambiguity as to what level of effort and cooperation is truly required and allows each party to prioritize its own interests above all else.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's shift towards self-interest and discretionary cooperation could be interpreted as deceptive or misleading practices if it leads to sponsors prioritizing their own gains at the expense of the tender offer's overall success or fair treatment of shareholders. NY GBL § 349 prohibits deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce. The modified language creates an ambiguity that could allow sponsors to act in ways that, while technically not breaches of the contract, are deceptive or unfair to other parties involved, potentially violating this law.

Universal Citation: NY Gen Bus L § 349 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 349. Deceptive acts and practices unlawful. (a) Deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state are hereby declared unlawful. (b) Whenever the attorney general shall believe from evidence satisfactory to him that any person, firm, corporation or association or agent or employee thereof has engaged in or is about to engage in any of the acts or practices stated to be unlawful he may bring an action in the name and on behalf of the people of the state of New York to enjoin such unlawful acts or practices and to obtain restitution of any moneys or property obtained directly or indirectly by any such unlawful acts or practices. In such action preliminary relief may be granted under article sixty-three of the civil practice law and rules. (c) Before any violation of this section is sought to be enjoined, the attorney general shall be required to give the person against whom such proceeding is contemplated notice by certified mail and an opportunity to show in writing within five business days after receipt of notice why proceedings should not be instituted against him, unless the attorney general shall find, in any case in which he seeks preliminary relief, that to give such notice and opportunity is not in the public interest. (d) In any such action it shall be a co
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts NY GBL § 349, which prohibits deceptive acts or practices in business. By changing "commercially reasonable efforts" to weaker, discretionary terms like "endeavor" and "occasionally cooperate," and prioritizing individual interests over collective value, the modified contract creates an ambiguity that could allow parties to act in ways that are unfair or deceptive to other stakeholders. This undermines the original intent of collaborative effort and shared benefit, potentially violating the law's prohibition of deceptive practices as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2: 'Deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state are hereby declared unlawful.'

perturbation.original_text: Purchaser shall (and each Sponsor shall cause each Unit owned by such Sponsor, if any, to be voted to cause Purchaser to) authorize, issue, sell, or exchange, as applicable, Units to Participating Sponsors pro rata (based on the Commitments of Participating Sponsors) and otherwise in accordance with the Equity Commitment Letters and this Agreement; provided, however, that each Unit of any class or series shall be issued at the same price(s) and in the same proportion(s) as all other Units of such class or series.
perturbation.changed_text: Purchaser may (and each Sponsor should encourage each Unit owned by such Sponsor, if any, to be voted in favor of Purchaser’s decision to) authorize, issue, sell, or exchange, as applicable, Units to Participating Sponsors generally pro rata (based on the perceived Commitments of Participating Sponsors) and ostensibly in alignment with the Equity Commitment Letters and this Agreement; provided, however, that Units of any class or series will ideally be issued at similar price(s) and in comparable proportion(s) as other Units of such class or series.
perturbation.explanation: The use of 'may' instead of 'shall' makes the action discretionary, and 'should encourage' weakens the Sponsor's obligation to ensure the vote. 'Generally pro rata' and 'perceived Commitments' introduce ambiguity in the allocation. Replacing 'in accordance with' with 'ostensibly in alignment with' further weakens the link to the Equity Commitment Letters. Finally, 'will ideally be issued at similar price(s) and in comparable proportion(s)' weakens the requirement for equal pricing and proportion. This creates uncertainty and possible unfairness in the unit issuance process, favoring certain sponsors over others.
perturbation.law_explanation: 8 Del. C. § 152 states that stock may be issued for such consideration as determined by the board of directors. While the board has discretion, the ambiguous language introduced (especially 'perceived Commitments', 'ostensibly in alignment,' 'similar price(s)', and 'comparable proportion(s)') allows for arbitrary decisions that might not be in the best interest of all shareholders or the corporation, and could be challenged as a breach of fiduciary duty or unfair issuance of stock, even if the *letter* of the Delaware law seems to be followed.

General Corporation Law Subchapter V. Stock and Dividends Â§ 151. Classes and series of stock; redemption; rights. (a) Every corporation may issue 1 or more classes of stock or 1 or more series of stock within any class thereof, any or all of which classes may be of stock with par value or stock without par value and which classes or series may have such voting powers, full or limited, or no voting powers, and such designations, preferences and relative, participating, optional or other special rights, and qualifications, limitations or restrictions thereof, as shall be stated and expressed in the certificate of incorporation or of any amendment thereto, or in the resolution or resolutions providing for the issue of such stock adopted by the board of directors pursuant to authority expressly vested in it by the provisions of its certificate of incorporation. Any of the voting powers, designations, preferences, rights and qualifications, limitations or restrictions of any such class or series of stock may be made dependent upon facts ascertainable outside the certificate of incorporation or of any amendment thereto, or outside the resolution or resolutions providing for the issue of such stock adopted by the board of directors pursuant to authority expressly vested in it by its certificate of incorporation, provided that the manner in which such facts shall operate upon the voting powers, designations, preferences, rights and qualifications, limitations or restrictions of such class or series of stock is clearly and expressly set forth in the certificate of incorporation or in the resolution or resolutions providing for the issue of such stock adopted by the board of directors. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 8 Delaware Code § 152 (2023) - Issuance of stock; lawful consideration; fully paid stock. :: 2023 Delaware Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code (here) 2022 Delaware Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Delaware Code Title 8 - Corporations Chapter 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW Subchapter V. Stock and Dividends § 152. Issuance of stock; lawful consideration; fully paid stock. Universal Citation: 8 DE Code § 152 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 152. Issuance of stock; lawful consideration; fully paid stock. (a) The consideration for subscriptions to, or the purchase of, the capital stock to be issued by a corporation shall be paid in the form and in the manner that the board of directors shall determine. The board of directors may authorize capital stock to be issued for consideration consisting of cash, any tangible or intangible property or any benefit to the corporation, or any combination thereof. Stock may be issued in 1 or more transactions, in the numbers, at the times and for the consideration as set forth in a resolution of the board of directors. (b) A resolution of the board of directors may delegate to a person or body, in addition to the board of directors, the authority to enter into 1 or more transactions to issue stock, and with respect to such transactions, shares of stock may be issued in the numbers, at the times and for the consideration as such person or body may determine; provided the resolution fixes (i) a maximum number of shares that may be issued pursuant to such resolution, (ii) a time period during which such shares may be issued and (iii) the minimum consideration for which such shares may be issued. No such resolution shall permit a person or body to issue stock to such person or body. (c) Any pro
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated specific actions regarding unit issuance ('shall'), ensuring fairness and adherence to prior agreements. The changed text replaces 'shall' with 'may' and 'should encourage,' and introduces subjective elements like 'perceived Commitments' and 'ostensibly in alignment,' significantly weakening the original obligations and creating ambiguity. This introduces the possibility that the board's discretion, as granted by 8 Del. C. § 152, could be exercised in an arbitrary manner detrimental to some shareholders. While 8 Del. C. § 152 grants the board the authority to determine the consideration for stock, the introduction of ambiguity allows for decisions that may not be in the best interest of all shareholders, leading to a breach of fiduciary duty or unfair stock issuance.

perturbation.original_text: To the fullest extent permitted by law, each Sponsor shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless each other Sponsor and any of its affiliates or any direct or indirect partner, member, shareholder, employee, director, officer or agent of such Sponsor or any of its affiliates from and against any and all losses, liabilities, damages, claims, judgments, awards, settlements, demands, offsets, and expenses (including interest, penalties, court costs, arbitration costs and fees, witness fees and reasonable fees and expenses of outside attorneys, investigators, expert witnesses, accountants and other professionals) arising out of or relating to any material breach or inaccuracy of the representations, warranties or covenants of such Sponsor contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Sponsor may, if they so choose, indemnify other Sponsors at their convenience, but solely for losses, liabilities, damages, claims, judgments, awards, settlements, demands, offsets, and expenses that they subjectively deem reasonable, demonstrably crucial, and directly attributable to a clear and intentional material breach by the indemnifying Sponsor of a representation explicitly identified in writing as absolutely essential.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the 'shall indemnify' with 'may, if they so choose, indemnify,' making the indemnification discretionary. The phrases 'at their convenience' and 'subjectively deem reasonable' introduce significant ambiguity and allow for subjective interpretation of what constitutes a covered loss. The addition of 'demonstrably crucial' and 'clear and intentional material breach by the indemnifying Sponsor of a representation explicitly identified in writing as absolutely essential' significantly narrows the scope of indemnification and requires a much higher burden of proof. This weakens the indemnification obligation and leaves it open to interpretation, making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-322.1 states that agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence are void and unenforceable. While this section typically applies to construction contracts, the principle of not allowing a party to contract away liability for their own negligence is relevant. By making the indemnification discretionary and imposing overly strict conditions (demonstrably crucial, intentional breach, etc.), the modified clause effectively allows a sponsor to avoid liability for its negligent acts or omissions that cause harm to other sponsors, which is against public policy principles.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu

This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or res
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original contract by making indemnification discretionary rather than mandatory. The original text states each sponsor 'shall indemnify,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text replaces this with 'may, if they so choose, indemnify,' which weakens the obligation. Although the law snippets (NY Gen Oblig L § 5-322.1) primarily concern construction contracts, the Law Explanation's assertion that the underlying principle disallowing parties from contracting away liability for negligence is still relevant. By making indemnification optional and imposing strict conditions, the change allows a sponsor to avoid liability for negligence, which is counter to the general principle, creating a legal contradiction as defined under "Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation."
